course_info.title: Influencer Marketing and Partnerships
prerequisites: DC201 - Social Media Strategy and Management

learning_objectives: Map the influencer marketing ecosystem including platforms, agencies, and measurement standards
Classify influencer tiers and explain the strategic trade-offs of each
Evaluate the role of authenticity and trust in influencer partnerships
readings: Levin, Influencer Marketing for Brands, Ch. 1-3
Influencer Marketing Hub, 'State of Influencer Marketing' (annual report)

lecture_notes.introduction: Influencer marketing is a $21+ billion industry built on the premise that peer recommendations are more persuasive than brand advertising. This course teaches you to identify, evaluate, negotiate with, and measure the impact of influencer partnerships.
key_concepts: Influencer tiers: nano (1K-10K), micro (10K-100K), macro (100K-1M), mega (1M+) and their trade-offs
Authenticity economics: why smaller influencers often drive higher engagement rates and conversion
Platform specialization: Instagram for lifestyle, YouTube for depth, TikTok for discovery, LinkedIn for B2B
FTC disclosure requirements: legal obligations for sponsored content transparency
The shift from influencer marketing to creator partnerships: co-creation vs. sponsored posts
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluating five influencer profiles across different tiers using audience quality analysis (fake follower detection), engagement rate calculation, and brand alignment assessment
setup_requirements: Social Blade or HypeAuditor free access

review.value: Nano and micro influencers typically achieve 3-6% engagement rates while mega influencers average 1-2%; smaller audiences are more engaged because the influencer-follower relationship feels more personal and less celebrity-like

The Federal Trade Commission requires clear and conspicuous disclosure of material relationships; #ad, #sponsored, or platform-native paid partnership labels must be visible without clicking 'more' or scrolling

learning_objectives: Use discovery tools and manual research to identify influencers aligned with brand values
Analyze audience demographics, engagement authenticity, and content quality
Apply a scoring framework to compare and rank potential influencer partners
readings: Levin, Influencer Marketing for Brands, Ch. 4-6
HypeAuditor, 'How to Detect Fake Followers and Engagement'

lecture_notes.introduction: Finding the right influencer is more important than finding the biggest one. A perfect brand-influencer alignment with a 20K-follower creator will outperform a mismatched partnership with a million-follower celebrity every time.
key_concepts: Discovery methods: hashtag research, competitor analysis, platform search, discovery tools, agency databases
Audience quality analysis: follower authenticity, demographic match, geographic relevance
Content quality assessment: production value, originality, brand safety, posting consistency
Brand alignment evaluation: values congruence, audience overlap, past brand partnerships
Red flags: sudden follower spikes (purchased), engagement pods, past controversies, undisclosed sponsorships
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running a complete influencer discovery and vetting process for a skincare brand: identifying 20 potential partners, screening for audience quality, scoring against brand criteria, and creating a shortlist of 5
setup_requirements: Discovery tools (Instagram search, TikTok discover, HypeAuditor)

review.value: A metric assessing what percentage of an influencer's followers are real, active humans in the target demographic, versus bots, inactive accounts, or geographic mismatches; tools like HypeAuditor estimate this score

Evaluating an influencer's content history for controversial statements, inappropriate content, or brand associations that could damage the partnering brand's reputation if the partnership becomes public

learning_objectives: Design an influencer marketing campaign with clear objectives, timeline, and content requirements
Write a creative brief that guides influencers while preserving authentic voice
Structure campaigns across awareness, consideration, and conversion objectives
readings: Levin, Influencer Marketing for Brands, Ch. 7-8

lecture_notes.introduction: The most effective influencer campaigns give creators enough direction to align with brand goals but enough creative freedom to maintain their authentic voice. Over-scripting kills authenticity; under-briefing wastes budget.
key_concepts: Campaign objectives: awareness (reach, impressions), consideration (engagement, traffic), conversion (sales, sign-ups)
Creative brief components: brand overview, campaign concept, key messages, do's and don'ts, deliverables, timeline
Balancing control and authenticity: mandatory elements vs. creative freedom zones
Content approval workflows: review rounds, revision limits, and timeline buffers
Multi-influencer campaigns: coordinating messaging across creators while maintaining individual authenticity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing a complete creative brief for a multi-influencer product launch campaign, then critiquing two example briefs (one over-prescriptive, one too vague) to identify the balance point
setup_requirements: Sample briefs for critique (provided on LMS)

review.value: Sections of the brief where the influencer has full creative control (storytelling approach, filming style, personal anecdotes) vs. mandatory elements (key messages, disclosure, product positioning); the ratio should favor freedom

A structured workflow: influencer submits concept, brand reviews within 48 hours, influencer produces content, brand reviews final draft within 24 hours, influencer publishes; clear timelines prevent bottlenecks

learning_objectives: Identify essential contract terms for influencer partnerships
Apply FTC disclosure guidelines to different content types and platforms
Navigate common negotiation scenarios including pricing, exclusivity, and usage rights
readings: FTC, 'Disclosures 101 for Social Media Influencers' (official guide)
Selected influencer contract templates and case law summaries (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Influencer partnerships are business relationships governed by contracts. Understanding contract terms, pricing models, and legal requirements protects both the brand and the creator, and prevents disputes that can derail campaigns.
key_concepts: Contract essentials: deliverables, timeline, compensation, usage rights, exclusivity, termination clauses
Pricing models: flat fee, performance-based (CPM, CPC, CPA), product exchange, revenue share
Usage rights: organic only, paid amplification rights, perpetual vs. time-limited, platform restrictions
FTC compliance: material connection disclosure, placement visibility, platform-specific requirements
Exclusivity and non-compete: category exclusivity periods and their cost implications
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reviewing a real influencer contract (anonymized), identifying missing or problematic clauses, then negotiating terms in a role-play scenario between brand and influencer

review.value: The legal permissions granted for how influencer-created content can be repurposed: organic posting only, paid media amplification, website use, print use, perpetual or time-limited; broader rights command higher fees

Any relationship between an influencer and brand that could affect the credibility of an endorsement: payment, free products, business relationships, family connections; all must be disclosed per FTC guidelines

learning_objectives: Define and calculate influencer marketing KPIs across the funnel
Build an influencer campaign report connecting activity to business outcomes
Calculate ROI and earned media value for influencer partnerships
readings: Influencer Marketing Hub, 'How to Measure Influencer Marketing ROI'

lecture_notes.introduction: Measurement is the difference between influencer marketing as a strategic investment and influencer marketing as an expensive experiment. Connecting influencer activity to business outcomes through proper tracking and attribution makes the case for continued investment.
key_concepts: Awareness metrics: reach, impressions, video views, brand lift surveys
Engagement metrics: engagement rate, saves, shares, comments, sentiment analysis
Conversion metrics: tracked links (UTM), promo codes, affiliate sales, attributed conversions
Earned media value: calculating the equivalent paid media cost of influencer-generated visibility
ROI calculation: (revenue attributed to campaign - total campaign cost) / total campaign cost
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete influencer campaign performance report: aggregating data from five influencer partners, calculating per-influencer ROI, earned media value, and overall campaign performance against objectives

review.value: An estimated dollar value of the visibility generated by influencer content based on equivalent paid media costs (what it would cost to buy the same reach and engagement through advertising); useful for comparison but not a revenue metric
Assigning unique discount codes to each influencer to track which creator drove which sales, providing direct conversion attribution; the simplest and most reliable influencer ROI measurement method

questions.question: Explain why micro-influencers (10K-100K followers) often deliver higher ROI than mega-influencers (1M+) for product-focused campaigns.
questions.answer: Micro-influencers deliver higher ROI because: (1) Higher engagement rates (3-6% vs. 1-2%) mean a larger percentage of their audience actually interacts with sponsored content. (2) Their audience perceives recommendations as more authentic and peer-like rather than celebrity endorsement. (3) Their fees are dramatically lower, so the cost per engagement or conversion is much less. (4) Their niche audiences often have higher purchase intent because followers actively chose to follow for specific expertise. The combination of lower cost, higher engagement, and stronger trust produces better conversion economics.

questions.question: What are three red flags that suggest an influencer has purchased fake followers or engagement?
questions.answer: Three red flags: (1) Sudden follower spikes visible in growth charts, showing thousands of followers gained in a single day without a corresponding viral post or media appearance. (2) Low engagement rate relative to follower count (e.g., 500K followers but only 200-500 likes per post), indicating that most followers are inactive bots. (3) Generic or spam comments (fire emojis, 'nice post!', irrelevant comments) from accounts with no profile pictures and few posts of their own, suggesting engagement pods or purchased comments.

questions.question: Describe the key components of an influencer creative brief and explain why over-scripting the brief reduces campaign effectiveness.
questions.answer: Key components: brand overview, campaign concept, target audience, key messages, mandatory elements (disclosures, product positioning), creative freedom zones, deliverables (number, type, platform), timeline, and approval workflow. Over-scripting reduces effectiveness because: (1) It destroys the authentic voice that makes influencer content persuasive; audiences can detect when content sounds like an ad. (2) It turns influencers into paid actors rather than trusted recommenders, undermining the entire value proposition. (3) It prevents influencers from using their expertise in knowing what resonates with their specific audience.

questions.question: What does the FTC require regarding disclosure of material connections in influencer marketing, and where must the disclosure appear?
questions.answer: The FTC requires that any material connection (payment, free products, business relationship) between an influencer and a brand must be disclosed clearly and conspicuously in every piece of sponsored content. The disclosure must appear: (1) Where it is hard to miss, not buried in a sea of hashtags or below the fold. (2) In the content itself, not just in bio links or account headers. (3) Before people need to click 'more' or expand the caption. (4) Using unambiguous language: #ad, #sponsored, or platform partnership labels are acceptable; vague terms like #partner or #collab without context are insufficient. Each piece of content needs its own disclosure, and stories/videos need spoken or text-overlay disclosure.

rubric.key: Names acceptable and unacceptable disclosure methods

major_assignments.title: Influencer Marketing Campaign Proposal
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete influencer marketing campaign proposal for a brand, including influencer identification and vetting, creative strategy, budget allocation, contract terms, and measurement framework.
objectives: Research and vet potential influencer partners using professional tools and criteria
Design a campaign strategy with clear objectives, creative direction, and timeline
Create budget projections and contract term recommendations
Build a measurement framework with KPIs and ROI projection
deliverables: Campaign strategy document (8-10 pages) with objectives, creative concept, and timeline
Influencer shortlist (5-8 creators) with vetting scorecards and partnership rationale
Creative brief for the top-choice influencer
Budget breakdown with per-influencer allocation and ROI projections
Measurement framework with KPIs, tracking methods, and reporting plan

criteria: Objectives are SMART and aligned with brand goals
Creative concept balances brand direction with influencer authenticity
Timeline is realistic with appropriate production buffers
Campaign addresses the full funnel from awareness to conversion
Vetting process is thorough and uses appropriate tools
Selection criteria are clear and consistently applied
Shortlist demonstrates strategic diversity in tier, platform, and audience
Rationale for each influencer is specific and compelling
Budget projections are realistic and based on market rates
Contract terms address all essential elements
FTC compliance is addressed throughout
ROI projections are grounded in reasonable assumptions
KPIs are defined for each funnel stage
Tracking methods are specific and feasible
ROI calculation methodology is sound
Reporting plan connects to strategic decision-making
Strategic campaign with clear objectives and creative vision
Rigorous influencer vetting with data-driven selection
Professional budget and compliance documentation
Comprehensive measurement framework connecting activity to outcomes

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on influencer marketing strategy and compliance

weekly_exercises.description: Weekly influencer analysis, brief writing, and campaign exercises

midterm_examination.description: Case study examination on influencer strategy and vetting

final_examination.description: Cumulative examination including live influencer evaluation and campaign design

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Guest speaker sessions with industry professionals cannot be rescheduled. More than 3 absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Campaign proposals submitted more than 5 days late will not be accepted.
course_policies.collaboration: Influencer research may be discussed among peers. All graded deliverables are individual work. Sharing campaign proposals, influencer lists, or creative briefs is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All influencer data must come from stated tools and platforms. Fabricating engagement rates or audience data is academic fraud. Brand partnerships referenced in case studies must be accurately represented.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need access to Instagram, TikTok, YouTube, and LinkedIn for influencer research. Social Blade and HypeAuditor free tiers are used for vetting. A laptop with reliable internet is required.
